title: The encoder-decoder Transformer
concept: Translation remains a classic encoder-decoder task: the encoder builds a language-independent representation of the source, and the decoder generates target text.

exercises.prompt: What classic AI task is best known for using an encoder-decoder architecture?
exercises.explain: Translation is the classic task that the encoder-decoder Transformer was originally designed to solve by mapping one sequence to another.

cards.title: Bridging the Language Gap
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers translate complex sentences from Japanese to French in milliseconds? It's not just a dictionary lookup; it's a team effort between two powerful AI components working in harmony.

cards.title: Encoder-Decoder Transformer
cards.body: The encoder-decoder Transformer is a dual-engine AI architecture. The Encoder reads and processes the source text into a core mathematical representation. The Decoder then reads this representation to generate the target translation word by word.

cards.title: The UN Translators
cards.body: Imagine a language translator at the UN. First, the listener (Encoder) listens to the speech and summarizes the core ideas in a notebook. Then, the speaker (Decoder) takes those notes and translates them out loud into a new language. They work together, but have totally separate jobs!

cards.title: Translating in Action
cards.body: When you type "How are you?" into a translation app, the Encoder processes the English sentence into numbers representing its meaning. The Decoder then uses those numbers to generate "Cómo estás?" in Spanish.

cards.title: They are Not Twins
cards.body: Many people think the encoder and decoder are just two copies of the same program. In reality, they are wired differently! The encoder looks at the entire input sentence at once, while the decoder is designed to generate text one step at a time, hiding future words from itself.

cards.title: Encoder vs. Decoder
cards.body: See how the two halves split the workload to turn input text into a completely different language.

left.text: Reads the entire input text and builds a deep, language-neutral map of its meaning.

right.text: Reads the map created by the encoder to generate the translated words one by one.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of the Encoder in a translation Transformer?

options.text: To build a language-independent representation of the source input
To predict the very last word of the translated output
To display the final translated text to the user
To delete grammar mistakes from the input text

exercises.explain: The encoder's primary job is to analyze the source language input and build a language-independent conceptual representation of it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The decoder can generate an accurate translation without any information from the encoder.
exercises.explain: The decoder relies directly on the representation built by the encoder to understand what meaning it needs to translate.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing component in the translation workflow.
exercises.sentence: The encoder analyzes the input, and the [blank] generates the output.
exercises.explain: The encoder processes the source text, and the decoder uses that processed information to generate the final translation.

exercises.prompt: Identify the output generated by a naive word-by-word translator instead of a context-aware Encoder-Decoder Transformer:

options.text: System A: "The bank of the river" -> "The money-store of the river" (Literal word-for-word translation error)
System B: "The bank of the river" -> "La orilla del río" (Correct contextual translation)
System C: "The bank of the river" -> "La rive du fleuve" (Correct contextual translation)

exercises.explain: Option A is generated by a rule-based dictionary translator that ignores context, whereas the Transformer understands the sentence's meaning to choose the right words.

exercises.prompt: How do the encoder and decoder work together during a translation task?

options.text: The encoder generates the translated words directly, while the decoder only checks for grammar errors.
The encoder creates a language-neutral map of the input, and the decoder reads that map to write the output.
The encoder translates the text into English, and the decoder translates English into the final target language.
Both parts do the exact same job on the input and output text at the same time.

exercises.explain: The encoder maps the input text to a language-independent vector space, while the decoder reads this space to generate the final translation word by word.
